metadata.summary: Select the correct load balancing algorithm (round-robin, least-conn, ip-hash, least-response-time, resource-based) based on request duration, server homogeneity, and state requirements.

content.title: Load Balancing Algorithm Selection
content.summary: Load balancing algorithms distribute traffic across backend servers. Static algorithms (round-robin, weighted round-robin, IP hash, random) ignore real-time server load; dynamic algorithms (least connections, weighted least connections, least response time, resource-based) adapt to server state. Algorithm choice is driven by request duration homogeneity, server capacity uniformity, and autoscaling behavior.

why.p: Algorithm choice on paper rarely survives real traffic. Round-robin can starve hot CPUs when request processing time varies widely. Least-connections is excellent for long-lived connections (WebSocket, database proxies) but adds connection-tracking overhead. IP hash gives sticky routing but creates uneven distribution with NAT/VPN clients and breaks autoscaling. Picking the wrong algorithm produces uneven load, degraded p99 latency, and unpredictable failover behavior.

LLMs default to recommending "round-robin is fairest" — this is only true when backends are identical and request processing time is uniform. Push for least-connections or response-time-aware algorithms unless those conditions hold.

list.item: Selecting an algorithm before writing any load balancer config.
Reviewing an existing config that exhibits uneven backend utilization or high p99 latency.
Autoscaling events are causing distribution problems (ip-hash + frequent scale events).
Traffic has variable request cost (some endpoints are fast, others are slow, long-running).
Session persistence is the primary concern — see lb-session-persistence for ip-hash and cookie-based approaches.
Tool-specific algorithm configuration (HAProxy balance directive, Nginx upstream) — see load-balancing-implementation.
Service mesh internal load balancing (Envoy/Istio) — those use distinct xDS-configured algorithms.

section.name: static-algorithms
section.p: Static algorithms assign traffic without real-time knowledge of backend state. They are simpler to implement and have zero tracking overhead, but they cannot react to load imbalances caused by variable-cost requests.

rule.statement: Use round-robin only when all backend servers have equal capacity AND request processing time is uniform (short-lived, homogeneous requests). Use weighted round-robin when servers have different capacities but request time is still uniform. Never use round-robin or weighted round-robin for workloads with variable-cost endpoints (mixed fast/slow API routes, large file uploads alongside simple reads).
rule.rationale: Round-robin distributes by connection count, not CPU cost. A server handling one long-running request alongside many short ones accumulates queue depth while receiving equal new connections. Least-connections handles this correctly at the cost of per-connection tracking.

rule.statement: Do not use IP hash in autoscaling environments or behind NAT/VPN. IP hash routes all traffic from a client IP to one backend; when backends scale in/out, all existing sessions redistribute. When clients share a NAT IP (corporate networks, mobile carriers), one backend receives disproportionate load.
rule.rationale: IP hash + frequent autoscale events causes constant wholesale re-distribution — the sticky property breaks on every scale event. NAT collapses many real users to one IP, creating a thundering-herd effect on a single backend. Cookie-based persistence handles both problems correctly.

example.title: Round-robin for uniform stateless API
example.note: Correct for equal-capacity backends with short, uniform-cost requests (e.g., REST API with consistent response times).

example.title: IP hash behind autoscaling group
example.note: IP hash breaks on scale events and creates uneven distribution with NAT. Use cookie-based sticky sessions instead.

section.name: dynamic-algorithms
section.p: Dynamic algorithms track real-time backend state (active connections, response time, resource utilization) to route new requests to the least-loaded server. They are the correct default for any workload with variable request cost.

rule.statement: Use least-connections (or weighted least-connections for heterogeneous capacity) as the default algorithm for any workload with variable request processing time: WebSocket connections, database proxies, long-running HTTP uploads/downloads, streaming endpoints, or mixed fast/slow API routes. Use least-response-time only when a load balancer health probe provides accurate per-backend latency measurements.
rule.rationale: Least-connections adapts to request cost naturally — a backend processing a slow request accumulates connection count and receives fewer new requests. Weighted least-connections extends this to heterogeneous fleets. Least-response-time is the most accurate but requires active probing; its benefit over least-connections is measurable only when backends have significantly different computational power or cache hit rates.

example.title: Least-connections for mixed-cost API backend
example.code:
backend api_servers balance leastconn option httpchk GET /api/health http-check expect status 200
default-server inter 10s fall 3 rise 2
server api1 10.0.1.1:8000 check server api2 10.0.1.2:8000 check server api3 10.0.1.3:8000 check
example.note: leastconn adapts to variable-cost endpoints automatically. Correct default for REST APIs with mixed endpoint costs.

example.title: Weighted least-connections for heterogeneous backends
example.code:
backend mixed_capacity_servers balance leastconn option httpchk GET /health
# weight proportional to server capacity (CPU/RAM) server large1 10.0.0.1:8080 check weight 100 server large2 10.0.0.2:8080 check weight 100 server small1 10.0.0.3:8080 check weight 50
example.note: Weight reflects relative capacity. leastconn distributes new connections to the server with fewest active connections relative to weight.

section.name: algorithm-selection-guide
section.p: Decision guide mapping traffic profile to algorithm:

list.item: Equal-capacity servers, short uniform requests, stateless → Round Robin
Different server capacities, short uniform requests, stateless → Weighted Round Robin
Variable-cost requests, any capacity mix → Least Connections (or Weighted Least Connections)
Performance-critical, active health probes available → Least Response Time
Session persistence needed → IP Hash (only for static server pools without autoscaling), or Cookie-based (preferred)
Heterogeneous server resources (CPU/RAM vary) → Resource Based (requires agent on each backend)

rule.statement: When adding a new load balancer to a production system, measure actual backend response time distribution before picking an algorithm. If p50 and p99 differ by more than 2x (evidence of variable-cost requests), default to least-connections. If they are within 20%, round-robin is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Algorithm decisions made without traffic data produce subtle load imbalances that are invisible until a backend falls behind and starts dropping requests under peak load. Measuring first eliminates guesswork.

checklist.check: Identify whether all backend servers have equal capacity (homogeneous vs heterogeneous fleet).
Measure or estimate request processing time distribution — is it uniform or variable?
Determine whether autoscaling is in scope — eliminates IP hash.
Determine whether clients share NAT/VPN IPs — eliminates IP hash.
Choose static algorithm (RR, WRR) for uniform short requests; choose dynamic (leastconn, WLC) for variable-cost workloads.
Verify the chosen algorithm is supported by the selected load balancer product.
Set connection weights proportional to actual server capacity for heterogeneous fleets.
Document the algorithm choice and its rationale in the architecture decision record.
Revisit algorithm when traffic doubles or fleet composition changes significantly.
